intro:
The Moabites were the people descended from Moab, son of Lot by his elder daughter (Genesis 19:37), a near kindred of Israel who settled the high plateau east of the Dead Sea between the Arnon and Zered rivers. Their land was fertile pasture, and their national god was Chemosh, whose worship at times included human sacrifice (2 Kings 3:26-27). Because they are blood relatives of Israel through Lot, the LORD forbade Israel to harass Moab during the wilderness journey (Deuteronomy 2:9).

Yet relations were marked chiefly by hostility. Refusing Israel passage and fearing them, Balak king of Moab hired Balaam to curse them (Numbers 22:1-6), and at Moab’s border Israel was seduced into idolatry and immorality at Baal-peor (Numbers 25:1-3). For this hostility the law excluded Moabites from the assembly of the LORD “to the tenth generation” (Deuteronomy 23:3-4; Nehemiah 13:1). In the time of the judges Eglon king of Moab oppressed Israel until Ehud delivered them (Judges 3:12-30), and Moab remained a frequent adversary subdued by David (2 Samuel 8:2) and later in revolt.

Against that backdrop shines one of Scripture’s great accounts of grace. Ruth the Moabitess, clinging to her mother-in-law with the confession “your people shall be my people, and your God my God” (Ruth 1:16), was welcomed into Israel, married Boaz, and became the great-grandmother of King David — and so an ancestor of the Messiah, named in Matthew’s genealogy (Ruth 4:13-17; Matthew 1:5). Her story shows that even one from an excluded people could be drawn into the covenant by faith.

The prophets pronounced repeated oracles against Moab for its pride and cruelty (Isaiah 15:1-9; Jeremiah 48:1-47; Amos 2:1-3), yet even there mercy is not wholly withheld — Jeremiah closes, “I will restore the fortunes of Moab in the latter days” (Jeremiah 48:47). The Moabites thus stand as both a warning against enmity toward God’s people and, in Ruth, a testimony that the gospel’s reach extends to the nations.
